title: The Culture Wars
summary: The culture wars are a framework for understanding the enduring conflict in American public life over values rather than economics: over abortion, religion, sexuality, guns, family, and what schools teach. The sociologist James Davison Hunter gave the idea its influential form in 1991, arguing that Americans were dividing into two moral camps, which he called orthodox and progressive, that cut across the older lines of denomination and class.
hook: There is no logical reason that a person's view on abortion should predict their view on guns, or on prayer in school, or on how a nation's history gets taught. These issues share no common principle. A library book and a rifle have nothing to do with each other. And yet, across American politics, they came to move together, as if pulled by a single hidden magnet. Tell a pollster where you stand on one, and you can often be sorted, with unsettling accuracy, onto a whole team. The puzzle is what that magnet is, and whether the war it implies is really fought by most Americans or only declared in their name.

beginner.theConcept:
Picture two families on the same street. They earn about the same. They both want good schools and safe roads. But ask them about abortion, or guns, or whether schools should teach children about sex, and they split hard. Here is the strange part. If you know where one family stands on abortion, you can often guess where they stand on guns, on prayer, and on almost everything else.

That bundling is what people mean by the culture wars. The phrase names a long fight in American life over values rather than money. It is a fight about family, faith, sex, guns, and what children are taught. A sociologist named James Davison Hunter gave the idea its modern shape in 1991. He argued that Americans were splitting into two moral camps, and that the split cut across the old lines of religion and class.
beginner.problemItSolves:
For a long time, people explained American politics through money. Rich against poor. Workers against owners. Which side you took was supposed to follow from your paycheck and your job.

That story left something out. By the late twentieth century, people with similar incomes were voting in opposite ways. A wealthy churchgoer and a wealthy professor might sit far apart. A factory worker and a college student might too. Money alone no longer predicted the vote.

The culture wars idea offered a different map. It said the deepest split was not about class but about values. What is a family? Whose authority counts, God's or the individual's? These questions, more than income, increasingly sorted people into camps. The idea explained a puzzle the money story could not.
beginner.howItWorks:
Hunter described two ways of seeing right and wrong. He called one side orthodox and the other progressive.

The orthodox side locates truth in a fixed authority outside the self, usually God or sacred tradition. The rules do not bend to fit the times. The progressive side locates truth more in reason, experience, and personal conscience. Old rules can be rethought as the world changes.

What made this new was that it cut across religions. A traditional Catholic, a traditional Protestant, and a traditional Jew might now stand together, against the more liberal members of their own faiths. The line no longer ran between churches. It ran through them. Two people could share a pew and land on opposite sides of nearly every heated question.
beginner.whatItExplains:
The idea explains why certain issues travel together. Abortion, guns, gay rights, and how sex and history are taught do not obviously belong in one package. Yet in American politics they moved as a bundle. A view on one came to predict a view on the rest.

It also explains why these fights feel so bitter. An argument about money can end in a deal, splitting the difference. An argument about whether life begins at conception cannot be split down the middle. When each side believes the other is attacking what is sacred, compromise can feel like betrayal.

It explains the shape of the flashpoints too. Abortion moved to the center of the storm after the Supreme Court's Roe v. Wade decision in 1973 made it legal across the country. School boards, courtrooms, and television became battlegrounds. The fight was less about any single law than about which vision of the good life the nation would carry.
beginner.whereItBreaksDown:
The idea can mislead when it makes the country sound split cleanly in two. Most Americans are not soldiers in a war. Many hold mixed views. A person can oppose late abortions and still want them legal early. A person can own guns and still want background checks. The loud extremes are real, but they are not most people.

Some scholars argue that the fiercest fighting happens among activists, donors, and politicians, not ordinary citizens. On this view, the war is hot at the top and much quieter in the middle. The image of two armies can hide all that shared ground.

The word war carries another risk. Calling opponents enemies can make peace harder. A description of a divide can end up deepening the divide it describes.
beginner.usedAndMisusedC:
The phrase is a weapon as much as a description. Politicians use it to rally supporters by naming an enemy. In 1992, Pat Buchanan told the Republican convention that a war for the soul of America was being fought. The line thrilled his backers and alarmed others. It cast political opponents as a threat to the nation itself.

Both sides can use it. Each can present itself as defending ordinary people against a hostile elite. Each can treat the other's deepest beliefs as mere prejudice.

Used with care, the phrase names something true. Americans really do disagree about the good life, not just about budgets. Used carelessly, it turns fellow citizens into opponents to be beaten rather than neighbors to be lived with. The same two words can describe a disagreement or declare a war.

general.theConcept:
The phrase carries an old echo. "Culture war" is a near-translation of the German Kulturkampf, the struggle of cultures that set Otto von Bismarck's new German state against the Catholic Church in the 1870s. When the sociologist James Davison Hunter borrowed the idea for America in 1991, he was naming something he believed had quietly reorganized the country's political life. The real division, he argued, no longer ran between Protestants and Catholics and Jews, or between rich and poor. It ran between two rival understandings of moral authority, and it cut straight through every religion and income group.

Hunter called the two sides orthodox and progressive. The orthodox anchor moral truth in a fixed, transcendent authority beyond human negotiation. The progressive locate it more in reason, personal experience, and a conscience free to revise inherited rules as conditions change. These are not simply the religious and the secular. A devout believer can hold progressive views, and a person of no faith can hold orthodox ones. What divides them is where final authority rests, and that question turns out to organize a startling range of disputes.

The concept matters because it claims to find coherence where none should exist. It says that abortion, sexuality, guns, and the content of a school lesson are not separate quarrels but surface expressions of one deep disagreement about how a society knows right from wrong.
general.problemItSolves:
Before this framework, the dominant way of explaining American politics ran through economics. The New Deal had sorted the country into a coalition of the less well-off against a coalition of the comfortable, and analysts read elections as contests over the distribution of wealth. Class was the master key. Where you stood depended on what you earned and what you did for a living.

By the 1970s and 1980s that key was sticking in the lock. Voters of similar means were splitting apart, and the split tracked something other than income. A prosperous suburban churchgoer and an equally prosperous professor seemed to want different countries. Working-class voters were peeling away over abortion, patriotism, and family. The economic model had placed them firmly on the left. The writer Thomas Frank later made a book-length puzzle of this, asking why so many people appeared to vote against their own material interest.

The culture wars idea answered by proposing a different axis entirely. The deepest political cleavage, it held, was not economic but moral. This reframing was powerful, and, as critics would note, it risked overcorrecting. In making values the master key, it could obscure how much economics still shaped who fought these battles and why.
general.howItWorks:
The mechanism runs through a claim about moral reasoning. Hunter argued that the orthodox and progressive positions are not two points on one scale but two incompatible starting points, which is why their disputes so rarely resolve. If moral truth is fixed and given, then abortion is the taking of a life, and no appeal to circumstance changes that fact. If moral truth is worked out through reason and lived experience, the same act is a hard question about a woman's freedom and the meaning of personhood. The two sides are not weighing the same evidence differently. They disagree about what counts as evidence.

This is why cultural conflicts resist the ordinary machinery of compromise. A budget dispute can be split, with each side taking half a loaf. A dispute over when life begins offers no half a loaf to take. The stakes are framed as absolute, which raises the emotional temperature and rewards the loudest voices.

The framework also describes how the two camps assembled. Activists, advocacy groups, and eventually the parties bundled the separate issues into packages. They taught voters to see them as a set. Over decades, the two major parties sorted themselves along the cultural divide. By the early twenty-first century, a position on one hot-button issue reliably predicted the others. That sorting was a historical achievement, not a fact of nature, and it happened at different speeds on different issues.
general.whatItExplains:
The framework's central payoff is that it makes sense of issue bundling, the otherwise baffling way unrelated questions travel together. There is no logical bridge from a view on gun ownership to a view on same-sex marriage, yet in American politics the two align with striking regularity. The culture wars account reads this as the visible trace of a single underlying divide about moral authority. Once you know whether someone anchors morality in fixed tradition or in evolving conscience, their scattered opinions snap into a pattern.

It also explains the peculiar intensity of these fights. Because each side experiences the other as attacking not a policy but a sacred order, the conflicts take on the character of existential struggle rather than negotiation. And it explains why certain flashpoints recur across generations in new costume. Think of the 1925 Scopes trial over teaching evolution, the long fights over school prayer and sex education, and recent disputes over what history and gender lessons belong in classrooms. On this reading they are the same war, fought on shifting ground. The school became a permanent front because it is where a society decides what to hand its children.

Abortion sits at the center of the story. After the Supreme Court's 1973 Roe v. Wade decision established a constitutional right to abortion, the issue became the organizing cause of a newly political conservative Christianity, and it stayed the defining line for decades. When the Court reversed that decision in 2022, returning the question to the states, it did not end the conflict but scattered it across fifty legislatures. The framework predicts exactly this. Because the disagreement is moral and not merely legal, a change in the law relocates the fight rather than concluding it.

There is a further, contested piece the framework helps illuminate: how the alliances themselves formed. Some scholars argue the religious right did not first mobilize around abortion at all. The historian Randall Balmer has argued that its earliest galvanizing cause in the 1970s was the defense of tax exemptions for racially segregated Christian schools, with abortion adopted as a rallying issue somewhat later. Others dispute that emphasis. The debate matters because it bears on whether the culture wars are timeless moral divisions or assembled political coalitions with specific, worldly origins.
general.whereItBreaksDown:
The framework strains hardest against the evidence about ordinary Americans. Its war metaphor implies two mobilized armies, but survey research has repeatedly found a public far more moderate and ambivalent than the metaphor suggests. The political scientist Morris Fiorina argued influentially that the American electorate is not deeply polarized on cultural issues. Rather, it is offered a polarized choice by activists, donors, and candidates who are themselves sorted. On this account, most citizens hold mixed and uncertain views, and the war is real chiefly at the top. How polarized the public truly is remains genuinely unsettled among scholars.

The concept strains again when it flattens each camp into a bloc. Real people combine positions the framework treats as opposite. A voter may hold traditional religious views and favor gun regulation, or embrace secular values and oppose late-term abortion. Sorting everyone onto one of two teams erases the large, cross-pressured middle where much of the country actually lives.

There is a deeper cost. By elevating values over economics, the framework can obscure how class and financial insecurity still shape these battles, who enlists in them, and which causes get funded and amplified. And the militarized language is not neutral. To name a disagreement a war invites people to treat opponents as enemies, which can help produce the very polarization the concept claims only to describe.
general.usedAndMisusedC:
"Culture war" is rarely a neutral description. It is almost always also a move in the fight it names. Pat Buchanan's 1992 speech to the Republican National Convention is the classic case. Declaring that there is a religious war going on for the soul of America, he cast the coming election as a battle for the character of the nation itself. The speech energized part of the Republican base and unsettled moderates, and it fixed the phrase in American vocabulary as a rallying cry rather than an analytic term.

The framing serves whoever deploys it. A politician who can persuade supporters that an election is a defense of their way of life against a hostile elite has raised the stakes past compromise and bound those supporters tighter. Both sides can play this game, and both do. Each can present itself as the embattled defender of ordinary, decent people, and each can dismiss the other's convictions as bigotry or as fanaticism.

Used carefully, the concept captures a real and important truth. Americans disagree about more than the size of government, and some of those disagreements reach genuinely incompatible visions of the good life. Used carelessly, it manufactures the enmity it claims merely to observe, turning citizens who share a country into camps who share only a battlefield. The responsible use holds both facts at once: that the divide is real, and that naming it a war is itself a consequential act.

advanced.theConcept: Hunter's thesis was, from the outset, an empirical wager as much as an interpretation, and the wager has not clearly paid. His 1991 argument held that a realignment of moral authority, orthodox against progressive, had displaced the denominational and class cleavages that once organized American religion and politics. The claim was bold precisely because it was falsifiable: if Hunter was right, one should observe the public itself dividing into two increasingly coherent and distant moral camps. Within five years, the sociologists Paul DiMaggio, John Evans, and Bethany Bryson tested that proposition against decades of survey data and found little support for it. Opinion on most social issues had not diverged into two poles; on several measures it had grown more consensual. The striking exception was abortion, where genuine and durable polarization did appear. The empirical heart of the culture wars thesis, in other words, survived at one point and failed at most others, which is the fact any serious use of the concept must now metabolize.
advanced.problemItSolves:
What the framework displaced, and what is at stake in displacing it, is the materialist account of political conflict that dominated mid-century analysis. In that account, descending from the New Deal order, the axis of politics was distributive: who gets what, with coalitions formed around economic interest. The culture wars thesis proposed that a second axis, roughly independent of the first, had become primary, that Americans were now sorted less by their relation to production than by their relation to moral authority. This was never a purely academic reframing. It carried a political payload, because it implied that the working-class coalition the left had assumed to be natural was in fact contingent and dissolving.

The sharpest version of the counterargument holds that the cultural axis is partly an illusion generated by the material one. Thomas Frank's polemic contended that cultural grievance functioned as a bait-and-switch, mobilizing working-class voters on abortion and guns while delivering economic policy that served the affluent. Others challenged the empirics: Larry Bartels argued that Frank overstated the defection of the white working class and understated its continued economic voting. The unresolved question is whether the culture wars are a distinct and autonomous domain of conflict or an ideological surface beneath which older distributive struggles continue by other means. The concept cannot settle this, but it makes the stakes of the disagreement legible.
advanced.howItWorks:
The mechanism the thesis proposes is a claim about the incommensurability of moral epistemologies, and its strength and its weakness lie in the same place. Hunter's orthodox and progressive positions are meant to be not merely different values but different theories of where moral knowledge comes from: revealed and fixed on one side, immanent and revisable on the other. If that is right, cultural disputes are structurally unlike distributive ones, because the parties share no common metric of value against which a compromise could be measured. This accounts elegantly for the affective heat and non-negotiability of the conflicts.

The difficulty is that the mechanism proves too much. If the divide were truly a clean split between two moral epistemologies, one would expect it to organize opinion consistently across all the issues it supposedly generates, and it does not. Attitudes on abortion, homosexuality, gender roles, and censorship have moved along different trajectories and at different speeds, several toward broad liberal consensus even as abortion stayed polarized. That pattern is better explained by a second mechanism the pure epistemological account underweights: political sorting, in which party elites and organized advocates bundled the issues and taught a mass public to associate them, so that the coherence of the camps is substantially an effect of partisan identity rather than its cause. On this reading the culture war is less the eruption of a pre-existing moral chasm than the ongoing construction of one.
advanced.whatItExplains:
Granting the empirical deflation, the framework retains real explanatory power, and specifying what it does and does not explain is the most useful work one can do with it. It does not explain a bifurcated mass public, because there is no such thing. What it does explain is the structure of elite conflict, and the mechanism by which a small number of genuinely non-negotiable issues can organize an entire political field out of proportion to the number of citizens who hold extreme views.

Abortion is the load-bearing case, and its exceptionalism is theoretically significant. It is the one issue on which the DiMaggio findings and their successors consistently locate real, stable mass polarization, and it is also the issue that most cleanly instantiates Hunter's incommensurability claim: the dispute genuinely turns on when a life warranting protection begins, a question no distributive compromise can reach. Andrew Hartman's history treats the whole period as an argument over the legacy of the 1960s, over whether the liberations of that decade fulfilled American ideals or betrayed them, and reads the curriculum fights, from the canon wars to sex education, as proxies for that larger reckoning over national self-understanding.

The framework further explains a dynamic a purely materialist account misses: the autonomy of symbolic politics. Fights over a monument, a textbook, a flag, or a bathroom carry stakes wildly disproportionate to their material consequences precisely because they are contests over recognition and status, over whose account of the nation is authoritative. This is why the 2022 reversal of Roe v. Wade redistributed the abortion conflict rather than resolving it: the underlying question was never only legal, so a legal settlement in one direction simply reopened mobilization on new ground. The concept's durable contribution is the recognition that democracies must process not only conflicts of interest, which are bargainable, but conflicts of ultimate value, which are not, and that they possess far weaker institutional machinery for the second than for the first.
advanced.whereItBreaksDown:
The critical literature has arguably overcorrected, and the strongest current position must guard against the deflationary account as carefully as against Hunter's original overreach. The finding that mass attitudes are not bimodally distributed does not by itself refute the claim that cultural conflict has become structurally central, because political outcomes are produced not by the median voter's ambivalence but by the mobilized and the organized. A public moderate in the aggregate can still be governed by an agenda set at the poles, and the intensity asymmetry, the fact that these issues activate donors and primary electorates with special force, gives the elite-level conflict real institutional consequences whatever the median citizen believes. Fiorina's formulation of a moderate public offered a polarized choice is a genuine tension, not a refutation.

A second limit concerns generality. The orthodox-progressive schema was built from and for a particular American religious landscape, and it travels poorly. Applied across borders it distorts, because the salient value conflicts elsewhere, over nationalism, immigration, secularism, and the accommodation of religious minorities, do not map onto Hunter's axis. Even at home the schema has aged. The rise of the religiously unaffiliated, the partial detachment of some conflicts from their theological moorings, and the migration of the fiercest disputes toward race, national identity, and gender have strained a framework organized around a Protestant-Catholic-Jewish moral realignment. The concept may describe the moment of its formulation better than it describes what came after, which is the characteristic fate of any framework that mistakes a moment for a permanent structure.
advanced.usedAndMisusedC:
The framing is itself a political instrument, and analyzing who benefits from its deployment is inseparable from analyzing the phenomenon. Hunter himself came to worry that naming a culture war helped call one into being, that the metaphor supplied a script into which ambivalent citizens were recruited to camps they had not chosen. The martial vocabulary performs a specific function: it converts negotiable disagreements into existential threats, which raises the value of mobilization and lowers the cost of intransigence. That payoff is available to entrepreneurs on every side, and the incentives of a fragmented media market, in which conflict is the reliably profitable product, reward its manufacture independently of any underlying shift in public opinion.

There is a symmetry worth stating plainly. Both the conservative and the progressive deployment of the frame present their own side as the beleaguered defender of decent ordinary people against a domineering elite, a coastal and secular establishment on one telling, a reactionary and theocratic movement on the other, and both convert their opponents' deepest moral commitments into pathology, bigotry on one account, fanaticism on the other. The analytically honest position refuses to treat either mobilization as simply true and the other as simply manipulation. It holds instead that the value conflicts are real and not reducible to false consciousness, and that the war framing is a contingent, consequential, and often cynical way of processing them, one that manufactures a share of the enmity it purports only to observe. Separating the genuine disagreement from the manufactured war is the whole of the analytical task, and the reason the concept remains useful even after its empirical claims have been heavily qualified.

foundation.theStory: The culture wars pose a distinctive kind of historical problem: a claim that the deepest divisions in a society are not about resources but about meaning. The United States spent the decades after the 1960s in recurring public conflict over abortion, religion in public life, sexuality, guns, and the content of schooling, and a powerful interpretation held that these scattered fights were really one fight, a struggle over which moral vision the nation would follow. Understanding the subject means holding two questions together: whether such a single underlying divide actually exists in the population, and why, even if it does not, a handful of intensely felt issues can dominate a nation's politics so completely.
foundation.theDebate: The central disagreement is about how deep the division really runs. One tradition, associated with James Davison Hunter and the historian Andrew Hartman, treats the culture war as a real and structuring conflict over moral authority and the meaning of the 1960s. A second tradition, associated with the political scientist Morris Fiorina and with survey researchers such as Paul DiMaggio and John Evans, argues that the mass public is far more moderate and ambivalent than the war metaphor implies, and that the genuine polarization is concentrated among political elites and on a few issues, above all abortion. The debate matters because it changes what the phenomenon even is: a genuine schism in the people, or a polarized menu offered to a moderate electorate.
foundation.whyItMatters: The culture wars framework is worth understanding because it shapes how Americans read almost every heated public conflict, from school-board meetings to Supreme Court nominations. Grasping it clarifies why certain issues bundle together, why they resist compromise, and why they recur across generations in new forms. It also opens onto adjacent subjects: the sorting of the political parties, the role of religion in a secular republic, the difference between conflicts of interest and conflicts of value, and the ways a metaphor can help create the reality it claims only to describe.
questions: If most Americans hold moderate or mixed views on issues like abortion and gun control, but the political parties offer sharply opposed positions, is the country actually divided, or is it being offered a division? How would you tell those two situations apart?

Why might a disagreement about money be easier to settle than a disagreement about when human life begins? What does that difference suggest about which political conflicts a democracy can resolve and which it can only manage?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The scholarly conversation runs across sociology, history, and political science, and its participants often reach opposite conclusions from the same events. James Davison Hunter, building on Robert Wuthnow's earlier account of a restructuring of American religion away from denominations and toward a liberal-conservative divide, framed the culture war as a clash of orthodox and progressive moral visions. Andrew Hartman later gave the conflict a narrative history centered on the contested legacy of the 1960s. Against this stands a deflationary camp: Morris Fiorina, with Samuel Abrams and Jeremy Pope, argued that the polarized-nation picture is largely a myth, while Paul DiMaggio and colleagues found little mass attitude polarization outside abortion. A third strand, represented by Thomas Frank and his critics such as Larry Bartels, debates whether cultural conflict is autonomous or a screen over enduring economic interest. The fault line throughout is whether the division is located in the public or in its elites.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is strong on some claims and genuinely underdetermined on others. Well established: attitudes on different social issues have moved along different paths and at different speeds, with several trending toward broad liberal consensus while abortion remained sharply divided. Also well established: political elites and the parties are far more sorted on cultural issues than the general public. Contested and unresolved: whether the mass public is meaningfully polarized at all, a dispute that turns partly on how polarization is measured and on which issues are counted. Also contested: the origins of the religious right, where Randall Balmer's argument that its first mobilizing cause was the defense of segregated Christian schools rather than abortion remains influential but disputed. The sharpest limits appear wherever the argument requires separating cultural from economic motivation, since the two are correlated in the same voters and cannot be cleanly pulled apart with survey data alone.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong position must resist two symmetrical errors: the alarmist picture of a nation cleanly split into two warring moral tribes, and the dismissive claim that the culture war is merely an elite invention with no real content. The most defensible view holds that the mass public is largely moderate and cross-pressured, that genuine and durable polarization is concentrated among elites and on a small set of non-negotiable issues led by abortion, and that this elite-level conflict nonetheless has large institutional consequences because intensity, not the median opinion, drives mobilization and agenda-setting. Defending this requires engaging the survey evidence honestly, conceding that abortion is a real exception to the moderate pattern, and explaining why a moderate electorate can still produce a polarized politics. It also requires treating the war metaphor with suspicion, since naming the conflict a war is itself one of the forces that hardens it, and a careful analyst distinguishes the disagreement, which is real, from the enmity, which is partly manufactured.
questions: Paul DiMaggio and colleagues found that abortion is the rare issue on which Americans genuinely polarized, while most other social attitudes did not. Why might abortion resist the moderating, consensus-building trend that other issues followed, and what does its exceptional status reveal about the limits of the culture war framework?

If the sharpest cultural conflict is concentrated among activists, donors, and politicians rather than the broad public, does calling it a war for the soul of the whole nation clarify what is happening or distort it? What would be gained or lost by describing it more narrowly?

research.topic: Most American social attitudes did not polarize into two camps; abortion is the durable exception
research.content: One of the more secure findings in the study of American public opinion is that, across the decades in which the culture war was said to be raging, mass attitudes on most social issues did not diverge into two increasingly distant poles. The influential 1996 study by Paul DiMaggio, John Evans, and Bethany Bryson, using long-running survey data, found stability or even growing consensus on most measures, with abortion standing out as the clear exception where genuine and lasting polarization appeared. Subsequent work, including the survey research summarized by Morris Fiorina and colleagues, has broadly reinforced the picture of a mostly moderate public. The consensus is on the general pattern, not on every issue or period; measurement choices and the treatment of party sorting continue to be debated.

research.topic: The migration of culture-war conflict onto new terrain of race, gender identity, and national belonging
research.content: A more recent line of scholarship examines whether the culture war has shifted its central battlegrounds since the framework was formulated in the early 1990s. On this account, as abortion, homosexuality, and school prayer either polarized or moved toward consensus, the most intense conflicts migrated toward disputes over race and history in the curriculum, transgender rights, immigration, and national identity, several of them detached from the explicitly religious framing Hunter emphasized. The claim is that Hunter's orthodox-progressive, Protestant-Catholic-Jewish schema fits the newer conflicts less well, and that the rise of the religiously unaffiliated has loosened the tie between the culture war and organized religion. This reframing is actively developing and contested; separating a genuine change in the conflict's content from a mere change in its most visible issues is methodologically difficult, and scholars disagree about how much continuity underlies the shift.

research.topic: Whether the aligned cultural camps reflect a pre-existing moral divide or were constructed by political sorting
research.content: A question at the center of the debate cannot be fully resolved by available evidence: whether the coherence of the two cultural camps reflects a real, pre-existing division in the population over moral authority, as Hunter argued, or was substantially manufactured by party elites and advocacy groups who bundled the issues and taught voters to associate them. Resolving it cleanly would require observing how Americans' scattered moral intuitions would have aligned in the absence of decades of elite issue-bundling and partisan sorting, a counterfactual that history did not run and cannot reconstruct, because the sorting and any underlying divide unfolded together in the same population. What can be responsibly inferred is that both forces operated: abortion shows a genuine and independent polarization, while the neat alignment of many other issues tracks partisan identity closely enough to suggest substantial construction. What must remain unknowable is the precise share each force contributed, since the causes are entangled in the single American case and cannot be varied independently.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Moral traditionalists and the religious right
comparativeNarrative.name: The Defense of a Sacred Order
comparativeNarrative.content: From this side, the culture war is defense, not attack. Believers watch practices they hold sacred, unborn life, the traditional family, a public square open to faith, being redefined by courts, universities, and media they cannot answer. Their intensity is not fanaticism. It is what people feel when a moral order they believe was given by God is dismantled in their children's schools and on their screens. Legal abortion and rapid changes to marriage arrived, on this view, imposed from above, and organizing to resist them is an act of conscience. The perspective sees what a purely secular account misses. For many Americans these are not policy preferences but the deepest commitments of a life, and treating them as mere prejudice is its own kind of contempt.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Expansion of Dignity and Freedom
comparativeNarrative.content: From this side, the same fights look like a long effort to extend freedom and equal standing to people long denied them. Access to abortion is about a woman's control over her own body and life. Recognition of gay and transgender people is about basic dignity. A curriculum that tells the whole national story, including its wrongs, is about honesty. Progressives feel the grief on the other side. But they experience the traditionalist stance as an attempt to use public power to enforce one religion's rules on everyone. The perspective captures what the other cannot. For those seeking recognition, the stakes are not abstract values but their own safety and place as full citizens, and appeals to a settled moral order have often been used to shut them out.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Skeptic's Correction
comparativeNarrative.content: Scholars who study public opinion see something smaller than either camp's self-image. Survey after survey finds most Americans near the middle, holding mixed and often uncertain views. Many accept some abortion limits and some abortion rights, some gun rules and gun ownership both. On this account the real division sits among activists, donors, primary voters, and politicians, who hand the moderate majority a stark choice it never asked for. The perspective corrects an assumption the two camps share, that the whole country is enlisted in their war. It also warns that the metaphor is not innocent. Naming a war can help recruit its soldiers, and treating a mostly moderate public as two hostile armies may deepen a divide that surveys suggest is thinner than it looks.

comparativeSummary: Held together, these vantages show why the culture wars are so hard to see plainly. The two combatant perspectives describe the conflict from inside deeply felt moral commitments. The social-scientific perspective describes it from outside, measuring a public that fits neither side's picture of a mobilized nation. The traditionalist and progressive accounts are not simply different feelings about the same facts. They rest on genuinely incompatible views of where moral authority lies and what counts as freedom, which is why their disputes resist compromise. The skeptic's correction does not refute them so much as move the intensity, from the population to its elites. No single account suffices, because the culture war is at once a real and unbridgeable clash over ultimate values and a partly manufactured spectacle offered to citizens who mostly live in the middle.

rabbitHole.label: Secularism
rabbitHole.reason: The culture wars turn on where moral authority rests, and the secular question, how far public life should be governed by religious commitments, is the axis Hunter's orthodox-progressive divide runs along; secularism is the framework for the argument the culture wars enact.

rabbitHole.label: Liberalism
rabbitHole.reason: The progressive side of the culture wars draws on the liberal tradition of individual conscience, personal autonomy, and rights against inherited authority; understanding that tradition is what makes the progressive position more than a set of policy preferences.

rabbitHole.label: Feminism
rabbitHole.reason: Abortion, gender roles, and sexuality are among the central fronts of the culture wars, and the feminist movement is both a driver of the changes traditionalists resist and a target of the conservative mobilization the framework describes.

rabbitHole.label: The Civil Rights Movement
rabbitHole.reason: The civil rights struggle supplied both a model of moral-political mobilization that later movements adapted and, on one contested account of the religious right's origins, the immediate backdrop of school desegregation against which conservative Christian politics first organized.

rabbitHole.label: The U.S. Constitution
rabbitHole.reason: The culture wars are fought largely through constitutional interpretation: the First Amendment's religion clauses govern prayer and public faith, the Second Amendment governs guns, and the fights over abortion and marriage were argued as questions of constitutional right.

rabbitHole.label: Populism
rabbitHole.reason: Both the culture wars and populism run on the framing of ordinary, virtuous people against a corrupt and hostile elite, and the two overlap wherever cultural grievance is mobilized as a revolt of the heartland against coastal, secular institutions.

rabbitHole.label: The Protestant Reformation
rabbitHole.reason: The Reformation was an earlier and world-shaping struggle over where religious authority rests, scripture and conscience against inherited institutional tradition, the same structural question that Hunter's orthodox-progressive divide re-poses in a modern political key.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: Many culture-war flashpoints, over monuments, flags, immigration, and how the national story is taught, are contests over national identity, the same terrain nationalism organizes; both concepts describe fights over whose account of the nation is authoritative.

reference.author: Hunter, James Davison
reference.title: Culture Wars: The Struggle to Define America
reference.annotation: The book that introduced the orthodox-versus-progressive framework into American public debate and made the culture war a category of analysis rather than a mere phrase. Essential for the entry's core claim about a realignment of moral authority cutting across denomination and class, and important precisely because Hunter framed it as a testable thesis about the public, which later research would challenge.

reference.author: Wuthnow, Robert
reference.title: The Restructuring of American Religion: Society and Faith Since World War II
reference.annotation: The sociological account, predating Hunter, of how American religious identity shifted from divisions between denominations toward a single liberal-conservative divide running through them. The source for the entry's claim that the culture-war alignment cut across churches rather than between them, and the intellectual foundation Hunter extended.

reference.author: Hartman, Andrew
reference.title: A War for the Soul of America: A History of the Culture Wars
reference.annotation: The leading narrative history of the American culture wars, reading them as an extended argument over the contested legacy of the 1960s. Essential for the entry's treatment of the school and curriculum as permanent fronts, and for the historical framing that connects Scopes-era fights to recent classroom battles.

reference.author: Fiorina, Morris P., with Samuel J. Abrams and Jeremy C. Pope
reference.title: Culture War? The Myth of a Polarized America
reference.annotation: The influential argument that the polarized-nation picture is largely a myth, and that a mostly moderate public is offered a polarized choice by sorted elites. The essential source for the entry's Where It Breaks Down analysis and for the elite-versus-mass distinction that reframes the whole debate.

reference.author: DiMaggio, Paul, Evans, John, and Bryson, Bethany
reference.title: Have Americans' Social Attitudes Become More Polarized?
reference.annotation: The empirical study that tested Hunter's thesis against decades of survey data and found little mass polarization outside abortion. The source for the entry's central factual pivot, that the culture-war thesis holds at one point and fails at most others, and for abortion's exceptional status.

reference.author: Frank, Thomas
reference.title: What's the Matter with Kansas? How Conservatives Won the Heart of America
reference.annotation: The widely read polemic arguing that cultural grievance mobilizes working-class voters against their economic interest. Useful for the entry's account of the class-versus-culture dispute, and valuable precisely because its thesis was contested, which sharpens the unresolved question of whether the culture wars are autonomous or a surface over economic conflict.

reference.author: Balmer, Randall
reference.title: Bad Faith: Race and the Rise of the Religious Right
reference.annotation: The concise statement of Balmer's argument that the religious right's earliest galvanizing cause was the defense of tax exemptions for segregated Christian schools, with abortion adopted later. The source for the entry's contested account of how the conservative coalition actually formed, presented as a live scholarly claim rather than a settled fact.

commerce.title: A War for the Soul of America: A History of the Culture Wars
commerce.author: Andrew Hartman
commerce.note: The best place to begin if you want the whole story as a narrative rather than a theory. Hartman writes accessible history for the general reader and walks through the actual battles, from the 1960s to recent classroom fights, so you meet the events before you tackle the arguments about what they mean.

commerce.title: What's the Matter with Kansas? How Conservatives Won the Heart of America
commerce.author: Thomas Frank
commerce.note: Read this if your first question is why culture came to outweigh money in so many people's politics. Frank is a vivid, opinionated writer with no jargon, and even though later scholars disputed his conclusions, his framing of the class-versus-culture puzzle is the clearest entry into that debate.

commerce.title: Culture Wars: The Struggle to Define America
commerce.author: James Davison Hunter
commerce.note: Go to the source once you want the original theory rather than summaries of it. Hunter is more careful and more sociological than the phrase he popularized suggests, and reading him directly shows how the orthodox-progressive idea was built and why it claimed to explain so many separate fights at once.

commerce.title: Jesus and John Wayne: How White Evangelicals Corrupted a Faith and Fractured a Nation
commerce.author: Kristin Kobes Du Mez
commerce.note: Read this to understand the conservative Christian side of the conflict from the inside of its own culture, its books, heroes, and images. Du Mez is a historian writing for a broad audience; her account is pointed and clearly comes from a critical standpoint, so pair it with a defender's account to hear both.

commerce.title: Culture War? The Myth of a Polarized America
commerce.author: Morris P. Fiorina
commerce.note: For the reader ready to test the whole premise against data. Fiorina's argument that the divided nation is largely a myth, and that the real polarization sits among elites, is closely argued and will change how you read every confident claim that America is split in two.

commerce.title: The Restructuring of American Religion: Society and Faith Since World War II
commerce.author: Robert Wuthnow
commerce.note: The book to read when you want the deeper sociological groundwork beneath the popular idea. Wuthnow is demanding but foundational, and his account of how American religion realigned from denominations toward a liberal-conservative split is what the culture-war thesis was built on.

popularCulture.title: "Culture war" as an everyday phrase
popularCulture.description: The two words are now a common label for almost any values fight. It can be a school-board meeting or an argument over an ad. The phrase points at something real. Many disputes really are about identity and belief, not budgets. But it can flatten very different quarrels into one story of two armies. It makes people reach for the language of battle, even when most of the country sits somewhere in the middle.

popularCulture.title: Pat Buchanan's 1992 Republican Convention address
popularCulture.description: Buchanan told the convention that a war for the soul of America was underway. The speech became the moment the phrase entered mainstream politics. It caught a real fear among conservatives that their way of life was under threat. Critics remember it as harsh and divisive. Both memories have lasted. Today the speech is the standard marker for when cultural conflict became open political strategy.

popularCulture.title: Inherit the Wind (1960, dir. Stanley Kramer)
popularCulture.description: This courtroom drama retells the 1925 Scopes trial over teaching evolution. It stages a clash between religious tradition and modern science. The film shaped how many people pictured that fight. It clearly favors the modern side and smooths the messy real trial into a clean morality play. Still, it catches the lasting shape of the culture wars. A single classroom becomes the stage for a fight over the whole society.

popularCulture.title: God's Not Dead (2014, dir. Harold Cronk)
popularCulture.description: This drama was a hit in the Christian film world. A college student defends his faith against a hostile atheist professor. It voices a real feeling among many conservative believers, that secular places look down on them. The story stacks the deck and makes its villains cartoonish. But it is a useful window into how one side sees itself: mocked, embattled, and fighting to be heard.

popularCulture.title: The Handmaid's Tale (2017–, Hulu)
popularCulture.description: This series comes from Margaret Atwood's 1985 novel. It imagines America remade as a religious dictatorship that strips women of their rights. It became a rallying image for progressives who fear where conservative religious politics could lead. Its red robes even showed up at real abortion protests. As a warning it is powerful. As a picture of actual American conservatives, it is a nightmare exaggeration.

popularCulture.title: "Political correctness" and later "woke"
popularCulture.description: These labels became weapons in the fight over language and identity. Each began as a way to name real changes in what counts as respectful speech. Then each hardened into an insult that opponents throw at one another. The words carry a genuine argument about respect and free speech. But used as slogans, they often replace the real debate. Each side can dismiss the other without ever engaging what is at stake.
